title: Ergotropy Protection via Cavity Detuning in Collective Open Quantum Batteries
abstract: This study investigates the performance and ergotropy protection of open collective quantum batteries subject to superradiant decay. By employing a passive spectral detuning strategy within an intermediate cavity, an optimal detuning value ($Δ^*$) is analytically derived and numerically verified to spectrally isolate the system and protect quantum coherence, achieving up to 1088% ergotropy improvement for single qubits and superextensive collective advantage for $N \ge 3$. Our analysis resolves a "non-Markovian paradox," revealing that maximizing ergotropy does not strictly require non-Markovian memory; rather, suppressing environmental memory via detuning optimally preserves coherence, which serves as the fundamental resource. Survival maps across different environments demonstrate that thermal noise dissipates coherence more severely than telegraph noise. Finally, we establish that collective amplification of the effective coupling ($g_{\rm eff} = g\sqrt{N})$ inevitably drives large qubit arrays into the ultra-strong coupling regime, providing a quantitative ceiling $N_{\rm max}$ on the validity of the Tavis-Cummings description and the current ergotropy protection protocol.
summary: This paper demonstrates that tuning the frequency of a cavity can protect the extractable energy in a collective quantum battery from environmental noise. It reveals that protecting quantum coherence is more important than relying on environmental memory and provides a scaling law for optimal protection as the number of qubits increases.
main_problem: How to protect the extractable work (ergotropy) in collective quantum batteries from energy loss caused by superradiant decay and environmental decoherence.
main_result: A passive cavity detuning strategy is proposed that spectrally isolates the system, achieving up to 1088% ergotropy improvement and resolving a 'non-Markovian paradox' by showing that suppressing environmental memory preserves coherence.
method: Analytical derivation using the Nakajima-Zwanzig projection operator and TCL2 approximation, complemented by numerical simulations using the QuTiP framework.
model_or_system: A collective quantum battery consisting of N identical two-level systems interacting with a single electromagnetic mode in a bosonic cavity, described by an extended T-Cummings model subject to superradiant decay.
key_observables: Ergotropy (E), BLP non-Markovianity measure (N), Collective Quantum Advantage (A), and effective decay rate.
important_parameters: Number of qubits (N), coupling strength (g), cavity detuning (Delta), cavity decay rate (gamma_0), and noise types (Ohmic/RTN and Thermal).
main_assumptions: The use of the Rotating Wave Approximation (RWA) and the validity of the TCL2 approximation, which may break down in the ultra-strong coupling regime.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows ergotropy dynamics over time; Fig 2 presents ergotropy survival heatmaps; Fig 3 illustrates the scaling of optimal detuning, the non-Markovian paradox, and the quantum advantage metric.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of ergotropy loss, derives an analytical optimal detuning strategy, explores the relationship between coherence and non-Markovianity, compares different noise environments, and establishes the limits of the model in the ultra-strong coupling regime.
why_interesting: It provides a practical, passive control mechanism (detuning) to mitigate superradiant decay in many-body quantum systems and offers a counter-intuitive insight into the role of non-Markovianity in quantum thermodynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Color scale indicates residual er- gotropy Eres from 0 (dark) to maximum (bright). The dashed white line represents the analytically derived detuning filter ∆∗(N) = g √

FIG. 1. Joint time evolution of residual ergotropy E(t) (solid lines, filtered at ∆∗; dashed lines, unfiltered at ∆= 0) and trace distance D(ρ1, ρ2) (BLP measure) for N ∈{1, 2, 3, 4} qubits. Left column: Environment A (RTN memory, λ = 0.05). Right column: Environment B (Thermal bath nth = 0.1, dephasing γϕ = 0.02). Parameters: γ0 = 0.05, g = 0.1, ωb = 1.0, ωc = 5.0, t ∈[0, 20]. All systems are initial- ized in the fully excited Dicke state |J = N/2, M = N/2⟩. The inverse correlation between memory backflow and er- gotropy retention under optimal detuning demonstrates the non-Markovian paradox.

FIG. 3. (a) Detuning scaling law: optimal ∆∗(N) vs N on a logarithmic scale. Black dashed line: analytical N 0.5 (Eq. 7). Solid lines: numerically optimal detuning with fitted ex- ponents βA = 0.3079, βB = 0.2061. (b) Non-Markovian para- dox for N = 2, Env A: filtered ergotropy Eres (red circles) and BLP measure N (blue squares) vs detuning ∆. The vertical line denotes ∆∗= 0.4472. (c) Collective quantum advantage A(N) vs N for Env A (blue circles) and Env B (red squares). The shaded region (A(N) > 1) indicates genuine quantum advantage.
